points.text: Instrument checkout flow: add timing logs around DB call, payment request, and response to pinpoint 5–10 s latency source.
Run EXPLAIN on the new SQL join; look for full scans or missing indexes causing slowdown.
Check DB lock contention or deadlocks introduced by the join; monitor pg_stat_activity or equivalent.
Verify payment API is idempotent: include a unique idempotency key per checkout to prevent double charges.
If idempotency not present, add a unique constraint on (order_id, payment_attempt) and handle duplicate errors gracefully.
Review client retry policy: implement exponential backoff with jitter and limit retries to avoid hammering the server.
Add circuit‑breaker or rate‑limit on payment calls; log each retry attempt for later analysis.
Reproduce the checkout flow under load in a staging environment and capture end‑to‑end latency metrics.
Instrument the checkout path with distributed tracing to pinpoint whether the delay is in DB, network, or service code.
Enable SQL query profiling (EXPLAIN ANALYZE) for the new join and look for full scans or missing indexes.
Check DB connection pool usage; high contention can add seconds to query time.
Add an idempotency key to the payment request so retries cannot create duplicate charges.
Configure client retry policy with exponential backoff and a max‑attempt limit to reduce load spikes.
Set per‑call timeout budgets and a circuit breaker around the payment service to fail fast on slowness.
If the join is the bottleneck, consider adding covering indexes or denormalizing the data.
Temporarily disable client retries to verify if duplicate charges disappear, then re‑enable with idempotency.
Monitor latency histograms before and after each change to confirm improvement.
Enable pg_stat_statements and log slow queries to capture the exact checkout SQL that now includes the new join.
Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on the captured query to see actual execution time, join order, and scan types.
Check that all columns used in the join predicates have b‑tree indexes; add missing indexes on foreign‑key columns.
Verify the plan uses index scans for the join; if it falls back to sequential scans, adjust statistics or create covering indexes.
Confirm the query’s WHERE clauses are selective; rewrite predicates or add partial indexes to improve selectivity.
Wrap the payment update in a single transaction with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE or use SERIALIZABLE isolation to avoid race conditions.
Add a unique constraint or upsert (ON CONFLICT) on the payment identifier to make the operation idempotent and prevent double charges.
Temporarily limit client retries and add exponential backoff while you fix the database bottleneck to reduce load spikes.
Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the new join query to spot missing indexes or full scans causing latency.
Monitor lock wait stats (e.g., pg_stat_activity, sys.dm_tran_locks) for contention on order/payment tables.
Check transaction isolation level; use READ COMMITTED instead of SERIALIZABLE if stricter level isn’t required.
Add a unique constraint or idempotency key on payment transaction ID to block duplicate charges on retries.
Wrap payment insert in a single atomic transaction and handle duplicate‑key errors gracefully.
Implement exponential backoff with limited client retries to reduce immediate contention spikes.
Use profiling tools like pg_stat_statements to track query latency spikes after the join deployment.
Instrument checkout to capture start/end timestamps and record p95/p99 latency.
Apply Little’s Law: concurrency = arrival_rate × avg_latency; ensure DB pool exceeds this.
Run the new SQL join alone, use EXPLAIN to compare its execution time to the previous query.
Add an idempotency key to payment API so retries do not create duplicate charges.
Limit client retries with exponential backoff and allow only one in‑flight checkout per user.
Budget timeout: client 2 s + service 1 s + DB 500 ms + network margin; enforce end‑to‑end limit.
Enable detailed request and DB latency logging around checkout to locate the slow SQL join.
Reproduce double‑charge by forcing concurrent retries and capture request timestamps.
Check the transaction isolation level; use SERIALIZABLE or REPEATABLE READ to avoid race conditions.
Add a unique constraint on (order_id, payment_attempt) to prevent duplicate charge rows.
Implement an idempotency token from the client and reject duplicate payments with the same token.
Profile the new join and ensure appropriate indexes to eliminate full table scans.
Adjust client retry logic to exponential backoff with a reasonable max‑attempt limit.
